summary:
Mureybet was a large Natufian and early Neolithic settlement on the middle Euphrates River in northern Syria, occupied from approximately 10,200 to 7000 BCE. The site is one of the key reference points for the transition from hunter-gatherer to agricultural life in the Levant, with successive occupation phases showing the gradual adoption of cultivated plants and the development of mud-brick architecture from earlier pit-house forms.

The upper levels of Mureybet contain some of the earliest evidence for the cultivation of rye and other cereals in the northern Levant, predating the emergence of typical Neolithic pottery assemblages. The site also preserves evidence for ritual activity, including the burial of aurochs skulls in house foundations, suggesting continuity between the symbolic practices of late hunter-gatherers and early farmers. Mureybet was submerged when the Tabqa Dam was built on the Euphrates, though it was excavated before inundation and its finds are preserved in Syrian and French museum collections.
